source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells

extracts.extract: Public records are the lifeblood of investigative reporting. Sunlight is the best disinfectant. Corruption hides in the shadows. You paid for it with your taxes. It should be yours (with exceptions). Journalism with a capital J is about holding the powerful accountable for their actions.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Accountability journalism and public records access are human institutional practices unaffected by LLMs

extracts.extract: As a journalist, it is your job to know this law better than most lawyers. Which law applies depends on which branch of government you are asking. In addition to documents and other kinds of information, FOIA also provides access to structured datasets.
extracts.relevance_rationale: FOIA law expertise and legal framework knowledge remain essential human competencies

extracts.extract: Please and thank you will get you more records than any lawyer or well-written request. Be nice. Be polite. And be persistent. Following up regularly to check on status of a request lets an agency know they can't ignore you. Hunting for records is like any other kind of reporting -- you have to do research. You have to ask questions.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Interpersonal relationship-building with records custodians is inherently human work

extracts.extract: When requesting data, you are going to scare the press office and you are going to confuse the agency lawyer. Request to have their data person on the phone.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Navigating agency politics to get data released requires human negotiation skills

extracts.extract: A good source of info? Records retention schedules, often required by law or administrative rule at an agency.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Knowledge of records retention schedules is institutional expertise unaffected by AI

extracts.extract: As a general rule, all assertions in a story based on data analysis should be reproducible. The methodology description in the story or accompanying materials should provide a road map to replicate the analysis.
extracts.chapter: Data journalism in the age of replication
extracts.notes: AP Stylebook commandment on replication
extracts.relevance_rationale: Reproducibility as a norm for data journalism is even more important when LLMs assist analysis

extracts.extract: Reproducible research -- replication -- is a cornerstone of scientific inquiry. Researchers across disciplines use methods to find new knowledge and publish it in peer reviewed journals. And, when it works, other researchers take that knowledge and try it with their own samples in their own locations.
extracts.chapter: Data journalism in the age of replication
extracts.relevance_rationale: Scientific replication principles apply regardless of whether analysis uses AI tools

extracts.extract: We journalists would be wrong less often if we adapted to our own use some of the research tools of the social scientists.
extracts.chapter: Data journalism in the age of replication
extracts.notes: Quoting Phil Meyer, New Precision Journalism (1971)
extracts.relevance_rationale: Adopting social science methods remains foundational regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Journalism must become social science in a hurry. To cope with the acceleration of social change in today's world, journalism must adopt systematic methods.
extracts.chapter: Data journalism in the age of replication
extracts.notes: Paraphrasing Phil Meyer
extracts.relevance_rationale: Need for systematic methods in journalism is amplified not diminished by AI

extracts.extract: The predominant way that data journalism gets done in a newsroom is through simple tools like Microsoft Excel or Google Sheets. Those simple tools, on their own, lack significant logging functions that automatically keep track of steps a data journalist took to reach a given conclusion. That means journalists using those tools have to maintain separate, detailed logs of what they did so any analysis can be replicated.
extracts.chapter: Data journalism in the age of replication
extracts.relevance_rationale: Spreadsheet logging limitations are partly solved by LLM-assisted notebook workflows that auto-document steps

extracts.extract: If the AP's replication rules are to be followed, journalism needs to become much more serious about the tools and techniques used to do data journalism. The days of 'point and click' tools to do 'quick and dirty' analysis that get published are dying. The days of formal methods using documented steps are here.
extracts.chapter: Data journalism in the age of replication
extracts.relevance_rationale: Formal documented methods remain vital but LLMs can now generate documentation from point-and-click workflows

extracts.extract: The Stylebook says at a minimum, today's data journalists should keep a log that details: The source of the data, making sure to work on a copy of the data and not the original file. Data dictionaries or any other supporting documentation of the data. Description of all steps required to transform the data and perform the analysis.
extracts.notes: AP Stylebook minimum standards
extracts.relevance_rationale: Logging standards endure but LLMs can auto-generate much of the documentation described here

extracts.extract: An increasing number of data journalists are switching to tools like analysis notebooks, which use languages like Python and R, to document their work. The notebooks, generally speaking, allow a data journalist to mix code and explanation in the same document. Combined with online sharing tools like GitHub, analysis notebooks seem to solve the problem of replication.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Notebooks remain relevant but LLM coding assistants now offer an additional path to documented, reproducible work

extracts.extract: Why should someone reading one of my articles believe my conclusions, if I don't provide the evidence that explains how I reached them? Simply saying what you've done is not as powerful as providing the means for others to repeat and build on your work.
extracts.notes: Quoting Peter Aldous, BuzzFeed
extracts.relevance_rationale: Transparency and evidence-sharing as trust-building principles are tool-independent

extracts.extract: If at all possible, an editor or another reporter should attempt to reproduce the results of the analysis and confirm all findings before publication.
extracts.notes: AP Stylebook internal replication standard
extracts.relevance_rationale: Internal replication is still vital but LLMs can now serve as a second pair of eyes for under-resourced newsrooms

extracts.extract: Journalism, unlike science, has no history of replication. There is no scientific method for stories. There is no standard research methods class taught at every journalism school. And journalism school isn't a requirement to get into the news business. In other words, journalism lacks the standards other disciplines have.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Journalism still lacks systematic replication standards and LLMs do not change this structural deficit

extracts.extract: Except for the largest newsrooms, most news organizations lack editors who could replicate the analysis. Many don't have a second person who would know what to do. Not having a second set of eyes in a newsroom is a problem. Having a data journalism team is an incredible luxury.
extracts.notes: Quoting Troy Thibodeaux, AP
extracts.relevance_rationale: Staffing gaps for replication persist but LLMs can partially fill the role of a second analyst

extracts.extract: The more critical the analysis is to the story, and the more complex that analysis is, that's going to push the bar on what is a sufficient methodology statement. And it could end up being a whole code repo in order to just say, this isn't black magic, here's how we got it if you're so interested.
extracts.notes: Quoting Troy Thibodeaux, AP — proportional transparency
extracts.relevance_rationale: Proportional transparency still applies but code repos can now be LLM-generated with better documentation

extracts.extract: Replication requires two things from you: What and why. What is this piece of code doing, and why are you doing that here and now? That you can copy and paste code from this book or the internet is not impressive. What is necessary for learning is that you know what a piece of code is doing and why you want to do that thing here.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding what code does and why remains critical even when LLMs generate the code

extracts.extract: Replication is a theoretical standard, not a requirement that every investigator duplicate his or her own work for every project. Giving enough information in the report to enable another investigator to follow in your footsteps is enough. Just telling enough to make replication possible will build confidence.
extracts.notes: Quoting Phil Meyer
extracts.relevance_rationale: Replication as theoretical standard with sufficient documentation is tool-agnostic

extracts.extract: One of the critical components of data analysis, especially for beginners, is having a mental picture of your data. What does each row represent? What does each column in each row signify? How many rows do you have? How many columns?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Mental model of data structure remains essential even when LLMs can describe datasets

extracts.extract: Is a zip code a number? When we think of data types, numbers are things we do math on. If the thing you want is not something you're going to do math on -- can you add two phone numbers together? -- then make it a character type. If you don't, most every software system on the planet will drop leading zeros.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding data types and semantic correctness is foundational regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: After loading the data, it's a good idea to get a sense of its shape. What does it look like? Use functions like glimpse() for column names and types, head() for the first rows, and explore interactively to understand the structure before analysis.
extracts.notes: Paraphrase of practical guidance on initial data inspection
extracts.relevance_rationale: Initial data inspection is now accelerable by LLMs that can profile datasets automatically

extracts.extract: A massive amount of data analysis involves grouping like things together and then doing simple things like counting them, or averaging them together. The pattern you'll use many times is: data, then group_by a column, then summarize with an aggregate function.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Group-by-summarize pattern endures but LLMs can now generate these aggregations from natural language

extracts.extract: The mean loan amount is much higher than the median loan amount in every county. There are some very large loans skewing the mean higher. Examining both the median -- which is less sensitive to extreme values -- and the mean -- which is more sensitive to extreme values -- gives you a clearer picture of the composition of the data.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding mean vs median and distribution shape is statistical literacy independent of tools

extracts.extract: Good practice -- one I'm going to insist on -- is that you put all your library steps at the top of your notebooks.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Library imports at top is a code organization practice LLMs handle automatically in generated notebooks

extracts.extract: Often the data you have will prompt questions that it doesn't immediately answer. The essence of mutate is using the data you have to answer a new question — calculating new metrics in a new field using existing fields of data.
extracts.notes: R/dplyr context but principle is tool-agnostic
extracts.relevance_rationale: Creating derived columns is now easily done via LLM prompts but understanding the concept still matters

extracts.extract: There's a mix of styles: 'Baltimore' and 'BALTIMORE' for example. R will think those are two different cities, and that will mean that any aggregates we create based on city won't be accurate. Use str_to_upper to standardize case before grouping.
extracts.notes: R-specific syntax but universal cleaning principle
extracts.relevance_rationale: Case standardization as cleaning principle endures but LLMs can detect and fix inconsistencies automatically

extracts.extract: You should never assume that missing values won't appear in a dataset. When classifying data with case_when or if_else, always account for blank or NA values as a separate category rather than letting them fall silently into another group.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Defensive handling of missing values is a universal data principle that LLMs may overlook if not prompted

extracts.extract: One of the most frustrating things in data is working with dates. Everyone has a different opinion on how to record them, and every software package on the planet has to sort it out. Consider using a dedicated date library (lubridate) to solve common problems before they start.
extracts.notes: R context; equivalent libraries exist in Python (pandas/dateutil)
extracts.relevance_rationale: Date parsing frustrations are substantially reduced by LLMs that can infer and convert date formats

extracts.extract: Filtering creates a subset of the data based on criteria. All records where the amount is greater than 150,000. All records that match 'College Park.' Something like that. Filtering works with rows -- when we filter, we get fewer rows back than we start with. Selecting simply returns only the fields named. So if you only want to see city and amount, you select those fields. Selecting works with columns. You will have the same number of records when you are done, but fewer columns of data to work with.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Filter vs select concepts endure but LLMs can now execute these operations from plain-language requests

extracts.extract: A general tip about using filter: it's easier to work your way towards the filter syntax you need rather than try and write it once and trust the result. Each time you modify your filter, check the results to see if they make sense. This adds a little time to your process but you'll thank yourself for doing it because it helps avoid mistakes.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Iterative filter refinement with verification is a thinking discipline that applies even with LLM assistance

extracts.extract: Any time you are given a dataset from anyone, you should immediately be suspicious. Is this data what I think it is? Does it include what I expect? Is there anything I need to know about it? Will it produce the information I expect? One of the first things you should do is give it the smell test. Failure to give data the smell test can lead you to miss stories and get your butt kicked on a competitive story.
extracts.chapter: Data Cleaning Part I: Data smells
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data skepticism and the smell test are fundamental journalistic instincts no AI replaces

extracts.extract: Some common data smells are: Missing data or missing values, Gaps in data, Wrong type of data, Outliers, Sharp curves, Conflicting information within a dataset, Conflicting information across datasets, Wrongly derived data, Internal inconsistency, External inconsistency, Wrong spatial data, Unusable data, including non-standard abbreviations, ambiguous data, extraneous data, inconsistent data.
extracts.chapter: Data Cleaning Part I: Data smells
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data smell checklist endures but LLMs can now automate detection of many of these issues

extracts.extract: Not all of these data smells are detectable in code. You may have to ask people about the data. You may have to compare it to another dataset yourself. Does the agency that uses the data produce reports from the data? Does your analysis match those reports? That will expose wrongly derived data, or wrong units, or mistakes you made with inclusion or exclusion.
extracts.chapter: Data Cleaning Part I: Data smells
extracts.relevance_rationale: Human verification through source calls and cross-referencing cannot be automated by LLMs

extracts.extract: We're also going to introduce a new argument to our function that reads in the data, read_csv(), called 'guess_max.' As R reads in the csv file, it will attempt to make some calls on what 'data type' to assign to each field: number, character, date, and so on. The 'guess_max' argument says: look at the values in the whatever number of rows we specify before deciding which data type to assign.
extracts.notes: R/tidyverse-specific but the principle of controlling type inference applies broadly
extracts.relevance_rationale: Type inference control is R-specific; LLMs can now handle type detection and conversion more fluidly

extracts.extract: Things that should be characters -- like state, city, name -- are characters (chr). Things that should be numbers (dbl) -- like amount -- are numbers. Date columns -- like date_approved -- are stored as dates. The id column is a good example. It read in as a number (dbl), which makes sense, because it really is just a string of numbers. But we'd never need to do math on these values; it wouldn't make sense to add two ids together. So it is probably best stored as a character. The opposite would be more problematic. If something that should be stored as a number we want to do math on was stored as a character, we couldn't actually use it to do math.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding semantic correctness of data types requires human judgment about what numbers mean

extracts.extract: Do the 135,073 NAs represent loans that weren't forgiven? Do they represent loans that might have been forgiven, but the data is simply missing the amount of money? We could check the documentation, which isn't particularly helpful. We could check the 'forgiveness_date' column, to see how many NAs it has. Before we decide to base a publishable finding on this column, we should call the custodian of the data to confirm our hypothesis of NA values in these columns, that they represent loans that have not been forgiven.
extracts.notes: Demonstrates journalistic duty to confirm meaning of missing values before publishing
extracts.relevance_rationale: Confirming meaning of NA values with data custodians is human reporting work LLMs cannot do

extracts.extract: It's good to be aware of all gaps in data, but they don't always represent a problem.
extracts.notes: Gaps may reflect real-world events (e.g. program expiry) rather than data errors
extracts.relevance_rationale: Contextual understanding of data gaps requires domain knowledge beyond what LLMs reliably provide

extracts.extract: Any time you are going to focus on a column for analysis, you should check for suspicious values. Are there any unusually large values or unusually small values? Are there any values that should not exist in the data?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Checking for suspicious outliers is a verification mindset that must persist even with AI-assisted analysis

extracts.extract: Those are all the same value, so at least this number is internally consistent. But is it correct? Did someone mistype it when entering the data? A call to the bank -- Bank of America in both cases -- or the companies could help resolve this mystery. Or a call to the SBA -- the owner of the data -- to ask about small values generally may help us understand if small values like this are suspicious or not.
extracts.notes: Internal consistency does not prove correctness; human follow-up needed
extracts.relevance_rationale: Internal consistency does not prove correctness; calling sources to verify remains irreplaceable

extracts.extract: Cleaning data is the process of fixing issues in your data so you can answer the questions you want to answer. Data cleaning is a critical step that you can't skip past. A standard metric is that 80 percent of the time working with data will be spent cleaning and verifying data, and 20 percent the more exciting parts like analysis and visualization.
extracts.relevance_rationale: The 80/20 cleaning-to-analysis ratio is shifting as LLMs dramatically accelerate data cleaning tasks

extracts.extract: Every developer, every data system, every agency, the all have opinions about how data gets collected. Some decisions make sense from the outside. Some decisions are based entirely on internal politics: who is creating the data, how they are creating it, why they are creating it. Is it automated? Is it manual? Are data normalized? Are there free form fields where users can just type into or does the system restrict them to choices? Your journalistic questions -- what you want the data to tell you -- is almost never part of that equation.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding data provenance and institutional context of collection is human interpretive work

extracts.extract: One of the most difficult problems to fix in data is duplicate records in the data. They can creep in with bad joins, bad data entry practices, mistakes -- all kinds of reasons. A duplicated record isn't always there because of an error, but you need to know if it's there before making that determination.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Duplicate detection endures as a concern but LLMs can now identify and flag duplicates more efficiently

extracts.extract: Remember the motivating question that's driving us to do this cleaning: What's the total amount of loans (at least in this tiny slice of data) for Arnold, MD in ZIP code 21012? We don't need the street_address field to answer that question. So we're not going to bother cleaning it. That's a good approach for the future. A good rule of thumb is that you should only spend time cleaning fields that are critical to the specific analysis you want to do.
extracts.notes: Clean only what your question requires
extracts.relevance_rationale: Question-driven scoping of cleaning work is editorial judgment that LLMs support but do not replace

extracts.extract: One rule you should follow when using this is: do not overwrite your original fields. Always work on a copy. If you overwrite your original field, how will you know if it did the right thing? How can you compare it to your original data?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Non-destructive editing by preserving originals is a universal data hygiene principle

extracts.extract: Using human judgement, you can say if you agree with the cluster. If you do, click the 'merge' checkbox. When it merges, the new result will be what it says in New Cell Value. Most often, that's the row with the most common result. Be careful\! If you merge two things that aren't supposed to be together, it will change your data in a way that could lead to inaccurate results.
extracts.chapter: Manually cleaning data with Open Refine
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual cluster-merge in Open Refine is being supplemented by LLM-powered entity resolution

extracts.extract: A question for all data analysts -- if the dataset is bad enough, can it ever be cleaned? There's no good answer. You have to find it yourself.
extracts.chapter: Manually cleaning data with Open Refine
extracts.relevance_rationale: Judging whether data is too broken to use is editorial judgment no tool can replace

extracts.extract: The next circle of Hell on the Dante's Inferno of Data Journalism is the PDF. Governments everywhere love the PDF and publish all kinds of records in a PDF. The problem is a PDF isn't a data format -- it's a middle finger, saying I've Got Your Accountability Right Here, Pal.
extracts.relevance_rationale: PDF extraction frustrations are substantially reduced by LLM-powered document parsing tools

extracts.extract: Unfortunately, harvesting tables from PDFs with Tabula is an exercise in getting your hopes up, only to have them dashed. We'll start with an example.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Tabula-specific frustrations are being displaced by LLM-powered PDF table extraction

extracts.extract: All things considered, that was pretty easy. Many - most? - electronic PDFs aren't so easy to parse. Sometimes you'll need to open the exported CSV file and clean things up before importing into R. Other times you'll be able to do that cleaning in R itself. Here's the sad truth: THIS IS PRETTY GOOD. It sure beats typing it out. And since many government processes don't change all that much, you can save the code to process subsequent versions of PDFs.
extracts.chapter: Cleaning up the data in R
extracts.notes: Saving processing code for repeated use on future PDF releases
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual PDF cleanup and code reuse pattern is being streamlined by LLM-assisted extraction pipelines

extracts.extract: Often, as data journalists, we're looking at data across time or at data stored in multiple tables. And to do that, we need to often need to merge that data together. Depending on what we have, we may just need to stack data on top of each other to make new data. If we have 2019 data and 2018 data and we want that to be one file, we stack them. If we have a dataset of cows in counties and a dataset of populations in county, we're going to join those two together on the county -- the common element.
extracts.notes: Distinguishes stacking (bind_rows) from joining (left_join etc.)
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding joins conceptually -- stacking vs merging on keys -- is foundational data literacy

extracts.extract: Rule 1 in joining data is having an idea of what you are expecting to get. So with a left join with applications on the left, I have 195,869 applications, so I expect to get 195,869 rows when I'm done.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Predicting expected row counts after joins is a verification discipline LLMs do not reliably enforce

extracts.extract: R is saying it can't join those two columns because one of them is a character column and the other is a double (number) column. When joining, the two columns need to have the same data type.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Type mismatch errors in joins are increasingly caught and fixed automatically by LLM coding assistants

extracts.extract: Joining datasets allows you to expand the range and sophistication of questions you're able to ask. It is one of the most powerful tools in a journalist's toolkit.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Joins as a powerful analytical tool remain conceptually important even if LLMs write the code

extracts.extract: Sometimes, governments put data online on a page or in a searchable database. And when you ask them for a copy of the data underneath the website, they say no. Why? Because they have a website. That's it. That's their reason. They say they don't have to give you the data because they've already given you the data, never mind that they haven't given to you in a form you can actually load into R with ease. Lucky for us, there's a way for us to write code to get data even when an agency hasn't made it easy: webscraping.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Government resistance to providing data in usable formats is an institutional problem unchanged by AI

extracts.extract: Why would we ever write code to grab a single table? There's several reasons: 1. Our methods are transparent. If a colleague wants to run our code from scratch to factcheck our work, they don't need to repeat the manual steps, which are harder to document than writing code. 2. Let's suppose we wanted to grab the same table every day, to monitor for changes. Writing a script once, and pressing a single button every day is going to be much more efficient than doing this manually every day. 3. If we're doing it manually, we're more likely to make a mistake, like maybe failing to copy every row from the whole table. 4. It's good practice to prepare us to do more complex scraping jobs.
extracts.notes: Four reasons to code scraping even for simple one-off tables
extracts.relevance_rationale: Transparency and reproducibility arguments for coded scraping apply equally to LLM-generated scripts

extracts.extract: A note about advanced scraping -- every site is different. Every time you want to scrape a site, you'll be puzzling over different problems. But the steps remain the same: find a pattern, exploit it, clean the data on the fly and put it into a place to store it.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Pattern-exploit-clean scraping workflow is increasingly handled by LLM agents that can adapt to site structures

extracts.extract: It's always a good idea to spot check the results, especially any values that look suspiciously high or low.
extracts.notes: Applied to scraped data but universally applicable
extracts.relevance_rationale: Spot-checking scraped results for accuracy is a verification step that persists regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: It's important to keep in mind that Census data represents people - you, your neighbors and total strangers. It also requires some level of definitions, especially about race & ethnicity, that may or may not match how you define yourself or how others define themselves.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Ethical awareness that Census data represents real people with contested categories is human responsibility

extracts.extract: But if the bars overlap, it means the difference between the two counties is within the margin of error, and the differences aren't statistically significant. So is the difference between Calvert and Montgomery significant? Nope. Is the difference between Howard and everyone else significant? Yes it is.
extracts.notes: Using error bar visualization to assess statistical significance in survey data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding margin of error and statistical significance is core data literacy unaffected by LLMs

extracts.extract: Visualizing data is becoming a much greater part of journalism. Large news organizations are creating graphics desks that create complex visuals with data to inform the public about important events. To do it well is a course on its own. And not every story needs a feat of programming and art. Sometimes, you can help yourself and your story by just creating a quick chart, which helps you see patterns in the data that wouldn't otherwise surface.
extracts.notes: Visualization as analytical tool, not just publication output
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data visualization landscape has expanded with LLM-generated charts but visualization importance endures

extracts.extract: Is this art? No. Does it quickly tell you something meaningful? It does. We're mainly going to use these charts to help us in reporting, so style isn't that important.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Using charts as analytical tools for reporting rather than art is a mindset unchanged by LLMs

extracts.extract: Doing data visualization well, and at professional level, takes time, skill and practice to perfect. Understanding it and doing it at a complex level is an entire class on its own. But simple stuff -- and even some slightly complicated stuff -- can be done with tools made for people who aren't data viz pros.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data visualization as a craft requiring practice and understanding persists in the LLM era

extracts.extract: Every chart needs a title, a source line and a credit line. Most need chatter (called description here). Really think about the title and description: the title is like a headline and the description provides some additional context. Another way to think about it: the title is the most important lesson from the graphic, and the description could be the next most important lesson or could provide more context to the title.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Chart annotation best practices endure but LLMs can now auto-generate titles, chatter, and source lines

extracts.extract: Another way we can look at the data is geographically. Is there a spatial pattern to our data? Can we learn anything by using distance as a metric? What if we merge non-geographic data into geographic data?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Spatial thinking about data patterns is an analytical instinct independent of tooling

extracts.extract: The degree of difficulty here goes from 'Easy' to 'So Hard You Want To Throw Your Laptop Out A Window.' And the curve between the two can be steep. You can learn how to scrape 'Easy' in a day. The hard ones take a little more time, but it's often well worth the effort because it lets you get stories you couldn't get without it.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Web scraping difficulty curve is flattening as LLMs can generate scraping code from page descriptions

extracts.extract: In programming, there's a principle called 'DRY' which stands for 'Don't Repeat Yourself.' If you find yourself copying the same code over and over again, with minor changes, it's better to find a way to avoid that.
extracts.relevance_rationale: DRY principle endures but LLMs generate functions and loops from natural language, lowering the barrier

extracts.extract: The bad news is that it's more complicated because it's more like survey data with a large sample. That means there's margins of error and confidence intervals to worry about.
extracts.notes: ACS estimates require attention to uncertainty, unlike decennial census hard counts
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding margin of error in survey data like ACS is statistical literacy that persists

extracts.extract: by examining the number of hospital beds per 100,000 people in each county, what regional geographic patterns can we identify? ... Each hospital exists inside of a county, so we're going to first calculate the total number of beds in each county.
extracts.notes: Demonstrates the standard move of normalising raw counts by population to make geographic comparisons meaningful
extracts.relevance_rationale: Per-capita geographic analysis endures conceptually but LLMs can now generate the normalization and mapping code

extracts.extract: But there's a problem. The column in our population data that has county names is called 'NAME,' and it has the full name of the county spelled out in title case ... If they're going to join properly, we need to clean one of them up to make it match the other.
extracts.notes: Key pre-join cleaning pattern: standardise case, remove extraneous text, trim whitespace so keys match across datasets
extracts.relevance_rationale: String matching for joins across datasets is increasingly handled by LLM fuzzy-matching capabilities

extracts.extract: We have two NAs after we join, for Queen Anne's County and Caroline County. That's not an error. There are no General Acute Care hospitals in those counties, according to our data ... So let's convert those values to 0 using replace_na().
extracts.notes: Post-join verification: explain why NAs appear and decide whether they represent missing data or true zeros
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding that NAs after joins may reflect real-world absence not errors requires domain judgment

extracts.extract: Baltimore City is still up there at the top, even when measuring by beds per 100k. But there are some surprising ones at the top, some of Maryland's smallest counties\! Allegany, Talbot, Somerset may not have that many beds, but they also don't have a lot of people.
extracts.notes: Illustrates how per-capita measures can surface surprising results driven by small populations
extracts.relevance_rationale: Interpreting geographic patterns and contextualizing surprises in data is editorial judgment

extracts.extract: So let's change the color scale to a 'log' scale, which will help us see those differences a bit more clearly.
extracts.notes: Log scales compress wide-ranging data so geographic patterns in the middle range become visible
extracts.relevance_rationale: Choosing log scales for visualization is a decision LLMs can now recommend and implement automatically

extracts.extract: Many of the data analyses that you do will be largely one-off efforts -- you're going to do the analysis and write the story and be done. ... But what happens when you have a long-running story, where you're going to update it every day, or every week? What changes when you're writing that code? 1. How will this run again without changing anything? 2. What questions do you have that have to be answered each time? 3. What changes when you have to repeat questions to changing data?
extracts.notes: Three design questions that distinguish a repeatable pipeline from a one-off analysis
extracts.relevance_rationale: Automating recurring analysis is now much easier with LLM-generated scheduled scripts and pipelines

extracts.extract: You could look at a county or state website, copy and paste some information into a file and then do some calculations, but there's a better way. Instead, let's build a system that goes out each day to retrieve the data, makes it easier to analyze and then calculates the percentage change in the number of people getting fully vaccinated between the latest date and a week earlier. And makes us a chart showing that.
extracts.notes: End-to-end automated pipeline: fetch, clean, compute, visualise -- replaces manual copy-paste
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual copy-paste vs coded retrieval tradeoff shifts further toward automation with LLM assistance

extracts.extract: The dashboard is a series of HTML pages placed on the main page via iframe ... But if you view the source on that URL, there's ... no data. It's being pulled in from another URL. If we want to automate getting this data, we'll need to find out where it lives. One big clue is in the composition of that URL: 'github.io.' Are these files (and maybe the data) already on GitHub?
extracts.notes: Technique for tracing dashboard data back to its raw source by inspecting URLs and following hosting clues
extracts.relevance_rationale: Reverse-engineering dashboard data sources is now assistable by LLMs analyzing page source

extracts.extract: when we read in that data, the vaccination_date column is formatted as a <chr> column, not as a date. Let's fix that using lubridate's handy mdy_hms function, which matches the format in the dataframe.
extracts.notes: Always check and fix date column types on import; lubridate functions match the source format
extracts.relevance_rationale: Date column type fixing is routine work LLMs handle automatically during data profiling

extracts.extract: Let's see if there's anything unusual in the data by counting the number of rows for each county ... There are totals for all of Maryland's jurisdictions, plus one for 'Unknown' and one that's NA. All but the last one have roughly the same number of records. What's going on with the NA records?
extracts.notes: Count rows per group as a quick data-quality check; unexpected categories (Unknown, NA) signal issues to investigate
extracts.relevance_rationale: Exploratory counting and anomaly detection can be accelerated by LLM-assisted data profiling

extracts.extract: They don't seem to pertain to any county (or no county), and it's not clear what these records represent. Let's remove them from our dataset
extracts.notes: Remove unexplainable NA rows only after investigating and confirming they add no analytical value
extracts.relevance_rationale: Deciding what ambiguous records mean and whether to exclude them requires editorial judgment

extracts.extract: Let's look at the most recent date, and that's something that takes on different meaning when we're talking about updating data. We need to make this generic so that every time we pull this up and run it, it's the most recent date at the top.
extracts.notes: Use dynamic date references (not hardcoded) so repeated runs always surface the latest data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Checking data currency and understanding update cadence requires human contextual awareness

extracts.extract: now is when we need to start asking ourselves -- what are the questions that are going to come up day after day? What about how this most current date compares to the previous day, or the previous week or month?
extracts.notes: Identifying the recurring editorial questions that drive the design of an automated analysis
extracts.relevance_rationale: Identifying recurring newsworthy questions to ask of updating data is editorial judgment

extracts.extract: What if we just ranked them? Where does this date rank? For that, we'll create a new column called Rank using mutate and we'll use a function called min_rank to rank them.
extracts.notes: Ranking the current day against all prior days to contextualise a single data point
extracts.relevance_rationale: Ranking and mutate operations are easily generated by LLMs from natural language descriptions

extracts.extract: How about the percent change between the latest date and one week ago? To do that, we need to find the latest date ... Luckily, if we have one date we can calculate another one by adding or subtracting days
extracts.notes: Standard percent-change calculation using date arithmetic to locate the comparison point
extracts.relevance_rationale: Percent change calculations across time periods are routine operations LLMs generate easily

extracts.extract: The first layer will be all the bars. The second layer will just be the latest, and we'll make that bar red. Then we'll add a point to the top of that line to really draw attention to it. Then we'll use ggrepel to label it. Then I'm going to add a smoothing line. That'll illustrate the trend clearly. The rest is labeling and adjusting the text to make it look more like a news graphic.
extracts.notes: Build up chart layers: all data, highlight latest, label, add trend line, style for publication
extracts.relevance_rationale: Multi-layer chart construction with highlighting is increasingly LLM-generatable from descriptions

extracts.extract: One thing we are missing? An automated summary. What if we programmatically wrote the description for this chart using the percent change calculation we did before? ... we're going to use a function called paste to merge some text together. We're going to paste together a sentence fragment, the percent change number and another sentence fragment together to form a sentence.
extracts.notes: Generate chart subtitle text programmatically so the narrative updates automatically with the data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Programmatic chart descriptions are now a natural LLM capability, extending this idea significantly

extracts.extract: This is going to be a story for months, if not years. So repeating this analysis is a must for a reporter covering health care in Maryland. We've set ourselves up to do this every week when the data comes out. We just open our notebook, go to Run > Restart R and Run All Chunks and sit back and watch as it does it all again. Then we go report.
extracts.notes: The payoff of automation: one-click re-run frees the reporter to do actual reporting
extracts.relevance_rationale: Automating recurring analysis for ongoing stories is now much more accessible via LLM pipelines

extracts.extract: when you have counties with a large number of cases, you'd use a different kind of scale called a log scale. It's a way of representing a very wide range of data in a more compact way
extracts.notes: Log scales make line charts readable when some series dwarf others
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding when to use log scales for skewed data is statistical reasoning independent of tools

extracts.extract: for a map, we can't have multiple days. We need a single day. Ideally, it would be the most recent date. We can get it using the max function.
extracts.notes: Use max(date) to programmatically select latest snapshot for map, avoiding hardcoded dates
extracts.relevance_rationale: Filtering to most recent date for maps is routine data wrangling LLMs handle from natural language

extracts.extract: The story didn't present any evidence of political meddling. Instead, it offered the results of several lines of data analysis that attempted to answer this central question: did red states get a bigger slice of the PPP pie than blue states? Mostly, it used basic descriptive statistics, calculating rates, ranking states and computing averages. But the data set it used also presents an opportunity to use two slightly more advanced statistical analysis methods to look for patterns: linear regression, to examine relationships, and a t.test, to confirm the statistical validity of an average between two groups.
extracts.chapter: Basic Stats: Linear Regression and The T-Test
extracts.notes: Progression from descriptive stats to inferential: rates and rankings first, then correlation and significance testing
extracts.relevance_rationale: Using multiple lines of data analysis to build a case without claiming causation is enduring methodology

extracts.extract: We can be a bit more precise by calculating a statistic called the correlation coefficient, also called 'r.' r is a value between 1 and -1. An r of 1 indicates a strong positive correlation. ... A correlation of 0 indicates no relationship. All r values will fall somewhere on this scale, and how to interpret them isn't always straightforward. They're best used to give general guidance when exploring patterns.
extracts.notes: r is a guide, not a verdict; interpretation requires context
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding correlation coefficients is core statistical literacy unchanged by LLMs

extracts.extract: Is this finding statistically valid? We can get a general sense of that by calculating the p-value of this correlation, a test of statistical significance. For that, we can use the cor.test function.
extracts.notes: After computing r, always test statistical significance with cor.test to check p-value
extracts.relevance_rationale: P-value calculation mechanics are automated by LLMs but understanding what p-values mean still matters

extracts.extract: p < .05 is accepted in many scientific disciplines -- and by many data journalists -- as the cutoff for statistical significance. But there's heated debate about that level, and some academics question whether p-values should be relied on so heavily.
extracts.notes: Acknowledges the contested nature of the .05 threshold; caution against mechanical reliance
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding p < .05 conventions and their limitations is statistical literacy independent of tools

extracts.extract: And to be clear, a low p-value does not prove that we've found what we set out to find. There's nothing on this graph or in the regression model output that proves that Trump's administration tipped the scales in favor of states that voted for it. It's entirely possible that there's some other variable -- or variables -- not considered here that explain this pattern. All we know is that we've identified a potentially promising pattern, worthy of additional reporting and analysis to flesh out.
extracts.notes: Critical epistemological point: statistical significance does not equal causal proof; patterns require further reporting
extracts.relevance_rationale: Low p-value not proving causation is a fundamental statistical reasoning principle

extracts.extract: A t-test can help us answer that question. It can tell us where there's a statistically significant difference between the means of two groups. Have we found a real difference, or have we chanced upon a statistical aberration?
extracts.notes: Use t-test when comparing means of exactly two groups to confirm the difference is not random
extracts.relevance_rationale: Running t-tests is easily automated by LLMs but knowing when to apply them still requires understanding

extracts.extract: This is a bit more promising. T is much stronger -- about 3 -- and the p-value is .002. Both of these should give us assurance that we've found something statistically meaningful. Again, this doesn't prove that Trump is stacking the deck for states. It just suggests there's a pattern worth following up on.
extracts.notes: Reinforces that even a strong t-test result suggests a pattern, not proof of causation
extracts.relevance_rationale: Interpreting t-statistics and p-values in context is human judgment work

extracts.extract: Turning unstructured text into data to analyze is a whole course in and of itself -- and one worth taking if you've got the credit hours -- but some simple stuff is in the grasp of basic data analysis.
extracts.notes: Frames text analysis as an extension of data analysis skills, not a separate discipline
extracts.relevance_rationale: Text analysis as a field has been transformed by LLMs which handle unstructured text far more capably

extracts.extract: What we want to do is to make the content column easier to analyze. ... We'll probably want to remove URLs from the text of the tweets since they aren't actual words.
extracts.notes: Strip URLs before tokenising text; they add noise to word frequency counts
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual text preprocessing like URL removal and tokenization is handled automatically by LLMs

extracts.extract: If you are trying to create a list of unique words, R will treat differences in capitalization as unique and also will include punctuation by default ... Fortunately, this is a solved problem with tidytext, which has a function called unnest_tokens that will convert the text to lowercase and remove all punctuation.
extracts.notes: unnest_tokens handles lowercase conversion and punctuation removal in one step
extracts.relevance_rationale: Case normalization and punctuation handling for text analysis is obsoleted by LLM text understanding

extracts.extract: Well, that's a bit underwhelming - a lot of very common (and short) words. This also is a solved problem in working with text data, and words like 'a' and 'the' are known as 'stop words.' In most cases you'll want to remove them from your analysis since they are so common.
extracts.notes: Remove stop words to surface meaningful content words in frequency analysis
extracts.relevance_rationale: Stop-word removal as a manual step is obsoleted by LLMs that inherently understand word significance

extracts.extract: The next step in text analysis is using ngrams. An ngram is any combination of words that you specify. Two word ngrams are called bigrams (bi-grams). Three would be trigrams.
extracts.notes: Move from single-word to multi-word analysis to capture phrases and richer patterns
extracts.relevance_rationale: N-gram frequency analysis is largely superseded by LLM semantic understanding of text

extracts.extract: There are some differences here, too, but also some potential challenges to doing an analysis. For one, there are variations of words like 'vaccine' that could probably be standardized - maybe using OpenRefine - that would give us cleaner results. There might be some words among our list of stop words that actually are meaningful in this context.
extracts.notes: Generic stop-word lists and raw token forms may not suit every domain; consider standardisation and custom stop words
extracts.relevance_rationale: Entity variation challenges in text analysis are better handled by LLMs but awareness of the problem endures

extracts.extract: Gauging the sentiment of a word can be heavily dependent on the context, and as with other types of text analysis sometimes larger patterns are more meaningful than individual results. But the potential with text analysis is vast: knowing what words and phrases that public officials employ can be a way to evaluate their priorities, cohesiveness and tactics for persuading voters and their colleagues. And those words and phrases are data.
extracts.notes: Sentiment lexicons are blunt instruments; value lies in aggregate patterns, not individual word scores
extracts.relevance_rationale: Context-dependence of sentiment is a real limitation that LLMs handle better than lexicons but imperfectly

extracts.extract: The number one sin of all early career data journalist is to get really, really, really attached to the analysis you've done and include every number you find. Don't do that. Numbers tell you what. Numbers rarely tell you why. What question has driven most people since they were three years old? Why. The very first thing to do is realize that is the purpose of reporting. You've done the analysis to determine the what. Now go do the reporting to do the why.
extracts.notes: Data analysis finds the what; reporting explains the why. Do not overload the reader with numbers.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Not falling in love with your analysis and letting the story lead is editorial discipline unchanged by AI

extracts.extract: The trick to writing a numbers story is to frame your story around people. Sometimes, your lead can be a number, if that number is compelling. Often, your lead is a person, a person who is one of the numbers you are writing about. Tell their story. Briefly. Then, let us hear from them. Let them speak about what it is you are writing about. Then come the numbers.
extracts.notes: Lead with a person, then pivot to numbers; the human story frames the data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Framing data stories around people rather than numbers is a storytelling principle independent of tools

extracts.extract: Using ratios instead of percents. Often, it's better to put it in counts of 10. 6 of 10, 4 of 10. It's easy to translate that from a percentage to a ratio. But be careful when your number is 45 percent. Is that 4 in 10 or 5 in 10? If a ratio doesn't make sense, round. There's 287,401 people in Lincoln, according to the Census Bureau. It's easier, and no less accurate, to say there's more than 287,000 people in Lincoln.
extracts.chapter: How to write about numbers without overwhelming with numbers
extracts.notes: Practical hierarchy: prefer ratios over percentages, round when appropriate, only use exact numbers when precision matters
extracts.relevance_rationale: Making numbers relatable through ratios and rounding is a communication skill unchanged by LLMs

extracts.extract: A critical question your writing should answer: As compared to what? How does this compare to the average? The state? The nation? The top? The bottom?
extracts.chapter: How to write about numbers without overwhelming with numbers
extracts.notes: Every number in a story needs a comparison point to be meaningful
extracts.relevance_rationale: Providing comparison context for numbers is core journalistic framing independent of tooling

extracts.extract: Sometimes ratios and rounding are not appropriate. ... Case counts are where an exact number is called for. You don't say that there are more than 70 cases in Lancaster County on the day this was written. You specify. It's 75. You don't say almost 30 deaths. It's 28. Where this also comes into play is any time there are deaths: Do not round bodies.
extracts.notes: Hard rule: never round death counts or case counts. Precision signals respect and accountability.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Knowing when exact numbers matter vs when rounding is appropriate is editorial judgment

extracts.extract: Then notice how they take what is a complex analysis using geographic information systems, raster analysis, the merging of multiple different datasets together and show that it's quite simple -- the averaging together of pixels on a 1-5 scale. ... Simplify, round, ratios: simple metrics, powerful results.
extracts.notes: Present complex methodology in its simplest accurate form; readers need the result, not the machinery
extracts.relevance_rationale: Translating complex analysis into plain language is a storytelling skill LLMs assist but do not replace

extracts.extract: The complexity comes when you realize the data you are dealing with represent real people's lives.
extracts.notes: Framing principle: data represents people, and publication has consequences
extracts.relevance_rationale: Recognizing that data represents real people is an ethical principle amplified not diminished by AI

extracts.extract: Because we were going to put this information in front of a big audience, Google was going to find it. That meant if we used our normal open door policy for the Googlebot, someone's mug shot was going to be the first record in Google for their name, most likely. ... And that record in Google would exist as long as the URL did. ... That was a problem because here are the things we could not know: Was this person wrongly arrested? Was this person innocent? Were the charges dropped against this person?
extracts.notes: Publication at scale changes the nature of public records; what was findable-in-principle becomes findable-in-practice
extracts.relevance_rationale: Considering searchability and downstream harm of published data is more urgent in the AI era

extracts.extract: The life of your data matters. You have to ask yourself, Is it useful forever? Does it become harmful after a set time? ... So, at the 60 day mark, we deleted the data. We had no way of knowing if someone was guilty or innocent, so all of them had to go.
extracts.notes: Design a data retention policy that reflects what you can and cannot verify about the records
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data lifetime considerations and when to remove published data are amplified by AI scraping

extracts.extract: We couldn't stop people from posting a mug on Facebook, but we didn't have to make it easy and we didn't have to put that mug front and center. So we blocked Facebook from using the mug as the thumbnail image on a shared link. And, after 60 days, the URL to the mug will throw a 404 page not found error. Because it's gone.
extracts.notes: Design friction into sharing: block thumbnails, expire URLs. Cannot prevent sharing but can limit amplification.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Minimizing harm by not publishing mugshots or harmful imagery is ethical judgment unchanged by AI

extracts.extract: What we didn't realize when we launched, and what we figured out after Pick the Perp, was that we had become data provider, in a sense. We had done the hard work of getting the data out of a website and we put it into neat, semantic, easily digestible HTML. If you were after a stream of mugshots, why go through all the hassle of scraping four different sheriff's office's horrible HTML when you could just come get ours easily?
extracts.notes: Any well-structured public data app becomes a de facto API; plan for downstream reuse
extracts.relevance_rationale: Recognizing journalists as data providers with downstream consequences is amplified by AI reuse

extracts.extract: What I want you to think about, before you write a line of code, is what does it mean to put your data on the internet? What could happen, good and bad? What should you do to be responsible about it?
extracts.notes: Ethics must be addressed at the design stage, before any code is written
extracts.relevance_rationale: Thinking ethically before coding is even more critical when LLMs make publishing data trivially easy

extracts.extract: Because you can put data on the web does not mean you should put data on the web. And there's a difference between a record being 'public' and 'in front of a large audience.'
extracts.notes: Crucial distinction: legal availability vs mass exposure. Scale changes the ethical calculus.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Distinction between public records and publishing structured databases is amplified by AI indexing

extracts.extract: So before you write the first line of code, ask these questions: This data is public, but is it widely available? And does making it widely available and easy to use change anything? Should this data be searchable in a search engine? Does this data expose information someone has a reasonable expectation that it would remain at least semi-private? Does this data change over time? Does this data expire? What is my strategy to update or delete data? How easy should it be to share this data on social media? How should I deal with other people who want this data? API? Bulk download?
extracts.notes: Comprehensive pre-publication ethics checklist covering discoverability, privacy, expiry, sharing, and downstream access
extracts.relevance_rationale: Ethical pre-publication checklist for data is more important given LLM-era scraping and aggregation
